mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of dual-mode operation in an operating system?

options.text: To improve CPU performance
To prevent user programs from interfering with system operations
To enable multitasking
To simplify application programming

mcqs.explanation: Dual-mode operation separates user mode (with restricted privileges) from kernel mode (with full privileges), preventing user programs from accidentally or maliciously damaging the system.

mcqs.question: Which of the following best describes what happens during a system call?

options.text: The program switches from kernel mode to user mode
The program switches from user mode to kernel mode
The operating system switches to a different process
The CPU clock speed increases to handle the request

mcqs.explanation: During a system call, the program executes a special instruction that causes a switch from user mode to kernel mode, allowing the OS to perform privileged operations on behalf of the program.

mcqs.question: What is the main purpose of a timer in operating system operations?

options.text: To display the current time to users
To prevent a process from monopolizing the CPU
To schedule file backups automatically
To measure program execution speed

mcqs.explanation: Timers are used to generate interrupts at regular intervals, allowing the OS to regain control and prevent any single process from using the CPU indefinitely, enabling preemptive scheduling.

mcqs.question: Which of the following operations would typically require switching from user mode to kernel mode?

options.text: Adding two integers in a program
Accessing a local variable on the stack
Reading from a file on disk
Calling a function within the same program

mcqs.explanation: Reading from a file requires accessing hardware resources (disk) which is a privileged operation that can only be performed in kernel mode through a system call.

mcqs.question: During context switching, what information must the operating system save and restore?

options.text: Only the program counter and stack pointer
Only the CPU registers and memory contents
The complete process state including registers, program counter, and memory management information
Only the contents of the files being accessed by the process

mcqs.explanation: Context switching requires saving the complete state of the current process (including all CPU registers, program counter, status word, and memory management information) and restoring the state of the next process to ensure seamless execution.
